title: Building a compost sieve from timber and wire mesh
subtitle: Making a riddle to grade finished compost and return large unfinished pieces to the heap
excerpt: A compost sieve, or riddle, separates fine finished compost from large undecomposed pieces that need more time. A simple timber frame with 12 mm galvanised wire mesh takes 30 minutes to build and lasts five to ten years. Sieved compost is uniform enough to use as seed compost, potting mix base, or fine lawn top-dressing.
sourceNotes: RHS composting guidance on sieving and grading. Soil Association compost quality guidance. WRAP Good Compost Guide on compost grades and applications.

glossaryTerms.definition: The gardening term for a compost sieve. A riddle has a coarser mesh (10 to 15 mm) than a fine garden sieve and is used specifically for grading finished compost.

The particle size range of sieved compost. Fine grade (under 6 mm) suits seed sowing and potting. Medium grade (6 to 12 mm) suits mulching and border use. Coarse grade (over 12 mm) is returned to the heap for further decomposition.

Steel wire mesh with a zinc coating that prevents corrosion. For compost sieves, use mesh with at least 0.9 mm wire diameter to resist the mechanical load of sieving. Heavy-gauge (1.2 mm) mesh lasts significantly longer with frequent use.

Fully decomposed compost that is stable, dark brown, crumbly, and smells of earth. Temperature has returned to ambient. Does not heat when wetted or turned. Sieving separates this fraction from partially decomposed material that needs more time.

content.text: Home compost bins produce compost with a mix of particle sizes.
Finished compost
graded through a
riddle

is suitable for seed sowing, potting mixes, and lawn top-dressing. Without sieving, large woody pieces and partially decomposed material in the mix make it unsuitable for these fine uses. A simple wooden riddle costs around 25 pounds in materials and gives years of use.

attrs.title: What to do with the oversize fraction

content.text: The material that stays on top of the sieve is not waste. It contains active microbes and partially decomposed organic material that will continue to break down. Return it to the active heap as starter material and add to the next layer of new additions. It typically takes two to three more months to fully decompose.
What you need
4 lengths of 50 x 25 mm softwood batten, 600 mm long, for the frame sides.
1 piece of 600 x 600 mm
galvanised mesh
with 12 mm apertures and at least 1.0 mm wire diameter. Smaller apertures (6 mm) for fine-grade sieving.
16 x 35 mm screws (galvanised or stainless) for the frame joints, and U-shaped staples or 20 mm staple gun staples for fixing the mesh to the frame.

Cut wire mesh leaves sharp edges that can cut through gloves. Fold the cut edges over by 10 mm before stapling to the frame to remove the hazard. Wear an FFP2 mask when sieving compost, as the process releases fungal spores and fine organic dust.
Building the frame

Cut four 600 mm lengths of 50 x 25 mm batten. Lay them in a square frame on a flat surface. At each corner, the end of one batten overlaps the face of the adjacent batten so all four sides are the same outer dimension.

Drill a pilot hole at each corner through the face batten into the end batten. Drive a 35 mm screw at each corner. The frame should be square: check by measuring both diagonals, which should be equal.
Cut the wire mesh to 600 x 600 mm. Fold 10 mm of each edge back over a batten or straight timber to remove the sharp wire ends.
Lay the mesh over the frame, stretched taut with no sag. Staple at 50 mm intervals around all four sides, pressing the mesh firmly against the batten face. The mesh should not bounce when pressed.
Using the sieve

Balance the sieve over a wheelbarrow. Tip compost onto the mesh in small amounts (1 to 2 litres at a time) and shake it side to side and back and forth. The fine fraction falls through and the coarse material stays on the mesh. For
fine compost grade
for seed sowing, shake over a second sieve with 6 mm mesh to remove any remaining medium-grade particles.
What done well looks like

The frame is square, rigid, and the mesh is taut with no sag. All wire edges are folded over and cannot cut through gloves. The finished sieve grades a full wheelbarrow of compost in 20 minutes. The fine fraction is uniform in particle size and free of sticks, root fragments, and undecomposed material.
What done badly looks like

The mesh was stapled too loosely and it sags and stretches under the weight of compost, letting large pieces fall through. The corners were not pre-drilled and one screw split the batten, weakening the joint. The wire edges were not folded and cut through the gloves on the first use.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Mesh keeps clogging with wet compost
attrs.cause: Compost is too wet: wet material smears into the mesh apertures rather than falling through
attrs.fix: Leave the compost to air-dry on a tarpaulin for a few hours on a dry day before sieving. Compost that crumbles when you squeeze a handful is the right moisture for sieving. Alternatively, dry the compost in a greenhouse for 24 hours before sieving.

attrs.symptom: Frame joints have pulled apart after a season
attrs.cause: Screws were driven in without pilot holes and the wood split, reducing holding strength
attrs.fix: Rebind the corners with 2 mm galvanised wire twisted tight as a temporary fix. Rebuild the frame using pre-drilled pilot holes at 80 percent of the screw diameter before driving each screw.

attrs.symptom: Sieving is very slow and tiring
attrs.cause: The sieve is being used with too large a load at once
attrs.fix: Use a maximum of 1 to 1.5 litres of compost at a time. Small batches sieve faster overall than large batches that need extensive shaking. A second person can help by feeding the sieve while the first shakes it.
